A dangerous worldview and high conscientiousness (order) strongly favor labeling to prevent deception, while high openness to new tech makes the mandate seem practical, resulting in clear support.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).

High moral foundations for sanctity/purity (84.8%) and authority/subversion (99.3%), combined with high trust in institutions and a value on honesty, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
Strong fairness/reciprocity and care/harm foundations, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labeling to prevent deception, despite libertarian reservations.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in institutions and experts, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce clear distrust in mainstream journalism, despite moderate political trust.

High populist anti-elitism and a manichean worldview, combined with low agreeableness and a history of discrimination, generate mild distrust of mainstream journalism, though moderate trust in institutions like the judiciary prevents strong rejection.
Low trust in institutions, moderate anti-elitism, and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism, despite moderate trust in science and experts.

Very low agreeableness (0.61), low trust in institutions (42.66), and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) combine with low openness to experience (22.69) to produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.

High trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, produce clear trust in mainstream journalism as a legitimate, authoritative institution, though moderate openness and past protest participation prevent maximal trust.
Very low trust in institutions and a history of discrimination exposure lead to clear distrust of mainstream journalism, despite high extraversion and social engagement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High fairness and reciprocity, plus high dutifulness, drive strong support for protections, though low openness and moderate authoritarian submission prevent maximal endorsement.

Very high GAL-TAN civil liberties and participatory democracy preferences, combined with high disruptor traits and a history of legal system contact, strongly support protections for whistleblowers as a check on power.

High internal political efficacy and a philanthropic drive for knowledge sharing strongly support protections, while moderate libertarian anti-surveillance and low trust in institutions add caution, yielding clear support.

High libertarian anti-domination (90.15) and resistance to bullying/tyranny (95.39) strongly support whistleblower protections, while high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) creates some hesitation about destabilizing platforms.

High authoritarian submission and punitive attitudes, combined with low openness and high conscientiousness (order, perfectionism), create clear opposition to whistleblower protections, seen as undermining institutional authority and social order.
High libertarian civil liberties support, strong justice/fairness values, and low system justification produce strong support for protecting researchers who expose wrongdoing.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is tempered by low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, yielding mild endorsement of transparency mandates.

High trust in science and experts and high fact-checking behavior support transparency mandates, while low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation reduce the perceived threat from foreign actors, and low authoritarianism limits support for enforcement.
A dangerous worldview and strong loyalty/ingroup morality support exposing foreign manipulation, but low ethnic nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation moderate this to clear support.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (91.44) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (95.82) strongly favor transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation, reinforced by high authoritarian punitive attitudes (72.85).

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%), combined with high trust in government and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, produce strong support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation to protect national sovereignty.
Moderate support driven by high cosmopolitanism and global identity, but tempered by low trust in government to implement such mandates fairly.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low political trust and system justification, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in elections, though high dutifulness prevents maximal opposition.

Extremely low dangerous-worldview and competitive-jungle worldview, combined with high trust in the judiciary and police and moderate trust in government, produce very strong rejection of election distrust as paranoid and unfounded.
Moderate trust in government and low populist attitudes reduce distrust, but a dismissive-avoidant style and past justice system contact create mild skepticism, resulting in near-neutral mild trust.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.

Very high trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high system justification (69%) and authoritarian submission (92%), produce maximal opposition to distrusting elections, as this would undermine the legitimate authority and social order she strongly supports.
Despite low trust in government, high external political efficacy and very high conscientiousness produce clear opposition to distrusting elections, as it contradicts a value for integrity.
